proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that 5% of the city's population (approx. 145,000 people) will voluntarily participate in a single-day event without significant logistical friction. The technical feasibility of the tracking app is low; the text admits the algorithm is 'similar' to existing footprint calculators but fails to address the critical technical challenge of distinguishing between walking, running, and other activities via smartphone sensors to accurately attribute GHG savings. Furthermore, the causal chain requiring government authorization to modify bus routes and compel companies to allow home office for a single day is operationally fragile and lacks a concrete implementation pathway.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is well-established globally, with the proposal explicitly citing existing programs in the US and Canada. The proposed innovation—a smartphone app to track steps and calculate savings—is a standard feature in existing health and sustainability applications (e.g., Google Fit, Strava, various carbon footprint apps). The proposal does not introduce a novel mechanism for behavior change or a new technical architecture for data collection, but rather repackages existing practices under a new event banner.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact estimates suffer from severe methodological flaws and logical inconsistencies. The short-term calculation (66.46 tCO2e/day) is plausible for a one-day event but is dwarfed by the long-term projections. The long-term forecast (105,937 tCO2e/year) erroneously attributes systemic infrastructure changes (e.g., a distributed solar law covering 1% of demand, 2% electric bus implementation) to the causal influence of a single awareness day. There is no empirical evidence or causal mechanism presented to support the claim that a one-day walking event will directly cause the approval of solar laws or the deployment of electric buses. The extrapolation to a global scale (39 million tCO2e/year) is mathematically derived but scientifically baseless given the lack of evidence for habit persistence or policy causality.

presentation.rationale: The proposal lacks scientific rigor in its presentation of data. It presents speculative forecasts as definitive outcomes (e.g., 'We forecast that it could increase by 10%'). The text contains numerous grammatical errors, inconsistent terminology, and broken references to images (e.g., 'Main value generation loops v1.0.png') that are not visible. The causal logic is presented as a linear progression without acknowledging confounding variables or the complexity of urban systems. The reliance on 'big data' from a single day to inform long-term infrastructure planning is presented without a statistical framework for validity.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal conflates a one-time awareness event with long-term systemic policy shifts. While the intent to reduce sedentary behavior and emissions is sound, the causal claims linking a single day of walking to the approval of solar laws and the deployment of electric buses are scientifically unsupported. The quantitative models rely on unverified participation rates and attribute macro-level infrastructure changes to micro-level behavioral nudges without a plausible mechanism.
key_concerns: Causal overreach: No evidence supports the claim that a one-day event will cause the approval of the 'Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law' or the implementation of electric buses.
Data validity: The proposed app lacks a defined methodology for accurately distinguishing walking from other activities to calculate specific GHG savings, risking significant measurement error.
Participation assumptions: The 5% participation rate is an unverified assumption that likely overestimates voluntary engagement for a single-day event without established incentives.

Systemic attribution: The proposal incorrectly attributes long-term infrastructure and policy outcomes (solar law, bus electrification) to the direct impact of the event rather than independent political or economic factors.
Technical feasibility: The proposal assumes government and corporate cooperation (modifying routes, mandating home office) without detailing the negotiation or legal mechanisms required.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on unrealistic institutional levers and regulatory assumptions. It assumes the government can unilaterally 'notify' companies to allow home office and 'arrange' transportation route modifications for a single day without a formal legislative or executive framework. Furthermore, the plan to have Google develop a custom app and collect 'big data' on citizen movement lacks a clear data governance, privacy, and procurement strategy, creating significant legal and operational barriers.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal's novelty lies primarily in the specific integration of a tracking app and the attempt to link short-term behavioral changes to long-term policy outcomes (e.g., solar law approval), but the core mechanism is not institutionally novel.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e/day) are plausible for a one-off event, the long-term impact projections are governance-heavy and speculative. The proposal assumes that a single awareness day will directly trigger complex legislative outcomes (e.g., 'Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law is approved') and infrastructure shifts (electric buses) without detailing the political advocacy or regulatory pathways required to achieve these systemic changes.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections but suffers from a lack of professional polish in its governance argumentation. It mixes high-level policy goals with operational details (e.g., ambulance costs) without a coherent implementation roadmap. The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') and the assumption of immediate government cooperation without a stakeholder engagement plan weakens the presentation of its feasibility.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance perspective, this proposal is an ambitious awareness campaign that underestimates the regulatory and institutional complexity required to execute its long-term goals. While the short-term event is feasible with local NGO support, the proposal's reliance on top-down government mandates for corporate behavior and its assumption that a single event will catalyze major legislative changes (solar laws, bus electrification) are not supported by realistic policy mechanisms. The data collection strategy also lacks necessary privacy and governance safeguards.
key_concerns: Lack of a clear legal or regulatory framework for the government to mandate or incentivize corporate home-office policies for a specific day.
Absence of a data privacy and governance strategy for the proposed smartphone app collecting citizen movement data.
Unrealistic assumption that a one-day awareness event will directly result in the approval of complex legislation (e.g., solar laws) and infrastructure changes.
No defined procurement or partnership model for the development of the tracking application by a major tech entity (Google).
Potential conflict between the goal of reducing public transport use (to encourage walking) and the need to maintain public transport efficiency and ridership.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'big bang' single-day event model requiring simultaneous coordination of government, media, sponsors, and 5% of a city's population, which presents massive operational risk. Critical dependencies are assumed rather than secured: the text states the government 'will have to arrange' route modifications and 'will notify' companies, without evidence of existing MOUs. Furthermore, the technical requirement for a custom Google-developed app to track routes and calculate GHG savings in real-time is a significant resource barrier for a volunteer team, and the reliance on voluntary home-office adoption by employers on a specific day is highly unpredictable.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day) with existing infrastructure like Buenos Aires' 'Ecobici' already in place. The proposal adds little novelty beyond aggregating these existing behaviors into a single day and proposing a data-tracking app, which is a standard feature in many existing fitness and carbon-tracking applications rather than a new innovation.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emissions reduction (66 tCO2e/day) is mathematically plausible for a single day, the long-term impact projections are highly speculative and likely inflated. The proposal assumes a 10% increase in recycling and a 2% implementation of electric buses are direct causal results of a one-day walking event, which lacks a logical operational bridge. The 'value generation loop' depends entirely on behavioral persistence post-event, which is historically difficult to sustain without continuous incentives, making the long-term scalability of the impact uncertain.

presentation.rationale: The proposal includes a Business Model Canvas and references specific local data, which is positive. However, the presentation suffers from significant structural issues: it mixes past tense ('We received the recognition') with future planning, contains broken image references (e.g., 'main-objections.png'), and relies on hypothetical government actions ('The government will notify...') as if they are confirmed steps. The financial section lists cost categories but provides no budget estimates, making the resource requirements opaque.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a high-risk, low-readiness event plan that assumes successful coordination of multiple independent stakeholders (government, private sector, media) without demonstrating existing commitments. While the intent to raise awareness is valid, the operational feasibility is low due to the lack of secured partnerships and the complexity of deploying a custom tracking solution. The long-term impact claims are disconnected from the immediate tactical actions, relying on optimistic behavioral assumptions rather than a robust change-management strategy.
key_concerns: No evidence of secured government authorization or MOUs, despite the proposal stating the government 'will' arrange routes and notify companies.
High dependency on a custom smartphone app developed by a major tech giant (Google) without a clear development budget or partnership agreement.
Unrealistic assumption that a single-day event will drive long-term policy changes (e.g., electric bus implementation, solar law approval) and behavioral shifts (recycling increases).
Lack of a concrete risk mitigation plan for low participation rates or logistical failures on the event day.
Ambiguous funding model: relies on 'sponsorships' and 'voluntary' participation without a clear path to covering the listed operational costs (security, health support, media ads).

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'big data' app developed by Google and government authorization to modify bus routes, which are significant external dependencies not under the team's control. Furthermore, the suggestion to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' contradicts the goal of reducing emissions, as public transit is far more efficient per capita than walking for long distances, making the core behavioral instruction impractical for the majority of the population who cannot walk 10-30km to work.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' days and bike-to-work initiatives is well-established globally (as the proposal itself admits by citing US/Canada examples). The novelty is limited to the specific data-tracking mechanism and the attempt to link it to corporate home-office policies, but the fundamental action is not new.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e/day) are modest, the proposal correctly identifies significant co-benefits for public health (reducing sedentary lifestyles) and social awareness. However, the long-term impact projections (e.g., 39 million tCO2e/year globally) are highly speculative and rely on unrealistic assumptions about habit formation and the scalability of a single-day event into a systemic change without addressing infrastructure barriers.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but it suffers from logical inconsistencies (e.g., advising people to avoid efficient public transit) and vague implementation details regarding the 'Google app' and government coordination. The tone is optimistic but lacks a grounded analysis of the daily realities of commuters in Buenos Aires.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a well-intentioned but disconnected campaign that misunderstands the daily logistics of urban mobility. While the push for walking and cycling is positive, the instruction to 'skip the bus' is dangerous advice for those with long commutes and ignores the reality that many residents rely on public transit out of necessity, not choice. The proposal risks alienating the very people it aims to help by suggesting they abandon reliable transport for a one-day event without addressing the safety, distance, or infrastructure gaps that prevent walking in the first place.
key_concerns: The instruction to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' is counter-productive and potentially harmful, as public transit is the most sustainable option for distances beyond walking range.
The proposal assumes a level of physical ability and proximity to work that excludes low-income workers, the elderly, and people with disabilities, raising issues of social equity and accessibility.
Reliance on a 'Google-developed app' and government route modifications are high-risk dependencies that are not actionable by the local team.
The safety of pedestrians and cyclists in Buenos Aires is not addressed; encouraging mass walking/cycling without traffic calming measures could lead to accidents.
The 'prize' model may incentivize gaming the system (e.g., walking in circles) rather than genuine behavioral change or commuting substitution.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that the government will mandate or strongly enforce 'home office' and modify public transport routes for a single-day event. The logistical burden of coordinating security, health support, and mass media for a voluntary event without a clear operational plan for the 'big data' app development (relying on Google) creates significant execution risks. The exclusion of those who cannot walk or bike due to disability or distance is not adequately addressed by the proposed 'home office' alternative, which requires employer cooperation not guaranteed in the text.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice, not a novel innovation. While the proposal attempts to add value through a specific app for tracking and a 'business model canvas' approach, the fundamental mechanism is a standard awareness campaign. The novelty is further diminished by the reliance on existing 'ecobici' programs and generic 'ecological footprint' algorithms rather than a new methodological or technological breakthrough.

impact.rationale: The proposal highlights a clear value conflict: it frames the event as a 'win-win' for health and environment but obscures the distributional consequences. The benefits (health, reduced stress, corporate cost savings) are unevenly distributed; those with sedentary desk jobs and the ability to work from home benefit most, while those in manual labor, service sectors, or with mobility impairments are effectively excluded or burdened. The long-term impact projections (e.g., 10% increase in recycling, 5% solar coverage) are speculative and lack a causal link to a single-day walking event, representing a 'halo effect' that may hide the lack of structural policy change.

presentation.rationale: The presentation attempts to be comprehensive with business models and impact calculations but suffers from a lack of critical self-reflection on equity. It presents a 'false neutrality' by assuming all citizens have equal capacity to walk, bike, or work from home. The text lists costs and benefits but fails to explicitly name the trade-offs regarding who bears the cost of the event (e.g., security, health personnel) versus who reaps the marketing benefits (sponsors, government). The data extrapolation to a global scale is presented with a confidence that the localized, voluntary nature of the pilot does not support.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal attempts to leverage a one-day event to drive systemic change, but it risks reinforcing existing inequalities by assuming a level of flexibility (home office, ability to walk/bike) that is not universal. While the intent to raise awareness is noble, the proposal hides the normative trade-off between individual voluntary action and the need for structural, inclusive policy. The projected long-term impacts are speculative and rely on a 'trickle-down' awareness model that may not translate to behavioral change for the most vulnerable populations who are often excluded from such initiatives.
key_concerns: Exclusion risk: The proposal does not account for individuals with disabilities, the elderly, or those with long commutes who cannot walk or bike, nor does it guarantee that 'home office' is an option for low-income workers.

Value conflict: The proposal prioritizes corporate and government branding (sponsors, 'big data' for business) over the immediate needs of the most vulnerable populations who may be disproportionately affected by climate change.

Unrealistic assumptions: The assumption that a single-day event will lead to a 10% increase in recycling or the approval of solar laws is a logical leap that ignores the complexity of policy implementation.

Data privacy and equity: The reliance on a smartphone app to track movement and calculate savings may exclude those without smartphones or data plans, creating a 'digital divide' in who gets recognized and rewarded.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unproven causal chain where a single-day event triggers systemic policy shifts (e.g., 2% electric bus adoption, 1% solar coverage) and behavioral changes (10% recycling increase) over five years. The assumption that a one-off 'awareness day' can force government infrastructure changes or alter long-term commuting habits without a sustained, multi-year intervention strategy is structurally weak. Furthermore, the reliance on a 'Google-developed' app and immediate government authorization for route modifications introduces significant execution risks that are not mitigated in the plan.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day) with no significant innovation in mechanism or delivery. The proposal attempts to differentiate itself through data tracking and 'big data' analytics for urban planning, but the underlying intervention—encouraging walking for a day—remains a standard awareness tactic rather than a novel systemic solution.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e/day) are real but negligible on a global scale, the projected long-term impacts are speculative and likely inflated. The proposal conflates correlation with causation, assuming that a single event will directly cause the approval of solar laws or the electrification of bus fleets. From a resilience perspective, this approach risks 'problem shifting' by creating a temporary reduction in traffic without addressing the structural need for permanent infrastructure or policy changes. The long-term societal impact depends entirely on whether the 'awareness' translates into durable habit formation, which is historically low for one-off events.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data to support its claims. However, the presentation of the long-term impact calculations is confusing and mathematically opaque, mixing specific event savings with broad policy forecasts (e.g., solar law coverage) without clearly distinguishing between the event's direct contribution and external factors. The narrative jumps between immediate event logistics and distant, hypothetical policy outcomes, making the causal logic difficult to follow for a long-term impact assessment.

A6.perspective_summary: This proposal treats a complex, systemic challenge (climate change and urban mobility) as a problem solvable through a single-day awareness event. While the immediate ecological benefit of reduced car use is positive, the long-term societal impact is questionable without a robust, sustained mechanism to convert temporary behavior into permanent structural change. The proposal's greatest weakness is its assumption that 'awareness' alone will drive the massive infrastructure and policy shifts required for genuine resilience, potentially leading to 'greenwashing' where the event is celebrated while underlying systemic issues remain unaddressed.
key_concerns: The causal link between a one-day event and long-term policy outcomes (solar law approval, electric bus implementation) is unsupported and likely represents a 'magic thinking' fallacy.
There is no strategy for sustaining the behavioral change beyond the event day; without ongoing reinforcement, the 'savings' will revert to baseline immediately.
The proposal assumes government and corporate cooperation (home office mandates, route changes) will happen automatically upon request, ignoring political and economic inertia.
The 'big data' value proposition is speculative; without a clear plan for how this data will be acted upon to change infrastructure, it remains a vanity metric.
The calculation of long-term impacts conflates the event's direct effect with broader national trends, inflating the perceived impact of the specific intervention.

conflicts.summary: While A1, A2, A4, A5, and A6 all score Impact at 2 or 3, there is a nuanced divergence regarding the *nature* of the impact. A1, A2, and A6 (scoring 2) view the long-term projections as scientifically baseless and a 'magic thinking' fallacy, arguing there is zero causal link between a one-day event and systemic policy changes. A4 and A5 (scoring 3) acknowledge the speculative nature but assign partial credit for identifying 'co-benefits' (public health, social awareness) and the potential for a 'halo effect,' even if the direct causal chain is weak. The conflict lies in whether the proposal's value lies in its flawed systemic claims (A1/A2/A6) or its immediate, albeit limited, awareness and health benefits (A4/A5).

clarification_requests.question: You scored Impact at 2 due to the lack of causal evidence for long-term policy shifts. If the proposal were re-scoped to explicitly frame the long-term policy outcomes (solar law, electric buses) as 'aspirational goals' rather than 'direct causal results' of the event, would your Impact score increase, and if so, to what level?

You scored Impact at 3, citing public health co-benefits. Given your strong concern that the instruction to 'skip the bus' is counter-productive and potentially harmful for long-distance commuters, how do you reconcile the net positive impact score with the risk of alienating or endangering the very populations the proposal aims to help?

You noted a 'value conflict' where benefits are unevenly distributed. If the proposal included a mandatory, funded alternative for those unable to walk or work from home (e.g., subsidized transit passes or specific accessibility accommodations), would your assessment of the 'distributional consequences' change enough to alter your Impact score?

clarification_responses.question: You scored Impact at 2 due to the lack of causal evidence for long-term policy shifts. If the proposal were re-scoped to explicitly frame the long-term policy outcomes (solar law, electric buses) as 'aspirational goals' rather than 'direct causal results' of the event, would your Impact score increase, and if so, to what level?
clarification_responses.answer:
Re-scoping the long-term policy outcomes (solar law, electric bus implementation) as 'aspirational goals' rather than direct causal results of the event would partially address the primary scientific flaw identified in my initial Impact evaluation: the attribution of macro-level systemic changes to a single-day behavioral nudge. By removing the claim of direct causality, the proposal avoids the logical fallacy of assuming a one-day event can trigger complex legislative and infrastructure shifts. This adjustment improves the internal consistency of the proposal's logic.

However, the Impact score would not increase to a high level (4 or 5) because significant empirical uncertainties remain regarding the *magnitude* of the actual, direct impact of the event itself. The short-term calculation (66.46 tCO2e/day) relies on a 5% participation rate which, while plausible for a major city event, remains an unverified assumption without pilot data. Furthermore, the proposal still lacks a robust mechanism to demonstrate that the 'awareness' generated translates into sustained behavioral change (habit persistence) beyond the event day, which is the critical variable for any long-term impact. The 'aspirational' framing clarifies the intent but does not provide evidence for the efficacy of the intervention in achieving even modest, sustained reductions.

Therefore, while the re-scoping corrects the causal overreach, the fundamental uncertainty regarding the intervention's efficacy and the lack of a validated behavioral change model prevents a substantial score increase. I would revise the Impact score from 2 to 3. This reflects a move from 'scientifically unsound due to causal errors' to 'plausible in intent but limited by unverified efficacy and lack of long-term behavioral data.' The feasibility and novelty scores remain unchanged as the technical and conceptual limitations persist.

clarification_responses.question: You scored Impact at 3, citing public health co-benefits. Given your strong concern that the instruction to 'skip the bus' is counter-productive and potentially harmful for long-distance commuters, how do you reconcile the net positive impact score with the risk of alienating or endangering the very populations the proposal aims to help?
clarification_responses.answer:
This is a crucial tension that lies at the heart of my evaluation. I reconciled the score of 3 by distinguishing between the *potential* of the core idea (promoting active transport and health) and the *flawed execution* of the specific instructions. The public health co-benefits (reducing sedentary lifestyles, mental health improvements) are real and significant for the segment of the population that *can* walk or bike safely—those living within 10-15 blocks of their work. For them, the impact is genuinely positive.

However, the instruction to 'skip the bus' is where the net positive is severely compromised. From the ground, this advice is not just impractical; it is exclusionary. It effectively tells low-income workers, the elderly, and people with disabilities that their daily commute is a 'choice' they can opt out of for a day, ignoring that for many, public transit is a necessity, not a luxury. If the event encourages people to walk 20km to work because they 'skipped the bus,' it risks physical injury, exhaustion, and alienation of the very communities most vulnerable to climate change.

The score of 3 reflects a 'mixed bag': high potential for health awareness among the able-bodied and proximate, but a significant negative impact on social equity and safety due to the 'skip the bus' directive. The proposal fails to differentiate between 'encouraging walking for short trips' (good) and 'discouraging transit for long trips' (harmful). To truly be a net positive, the messaging must shift from 'avoid transit' to 'walk or bike for short trips, and use transit for long ones,' while advocating for better transit efficiency rather than its avoidance. As it stands, the risk of alienating the population and promoting unsafe behaviors keeps the impact score from being higher, despite the valid health goals.

clarification_responses.question: You noted a 'value conflict' where benefits are unevenly distributed. If the proposal included a mandatory, funded alternative for those unable to walk or work from home (e.g., subsidized transit passes or specific accessibility accommodations), would your assessment of the 'distributional consequences' change enough to alter your Impact score?
clarification_responses.answer:
Yes, the inclusion of a mandatory, funded alternative for those unable to walk or work from home would fundamentally alter the distributional consequences and justify revising the Impact score. My previous score of 3 was grounded in the proposal's 'false neutrality'—the assumption that all citizens possess the physical ability to walk/bike or the economic privilege to work from home. This created a high exclusion risk where the benefits (health, corporate savings, brand prestige) accrued to the able-bodied and affluent, while the costs (mobility constraints, lack of participation options) fell on the disabled, elderly, and low-income service workers.

If the proposal explicitly mandates and funds alternatives (e.g., subsidized transit passes, accessible shuttle services, or guaranteed paid leave for those who cannot participate), it shifts the normative framework from 'voluntary individual action' to 'inclusive structural support.' This directly addresses the value conflict I identified: it ensures that the burden of the transition is not borne disproportionately by the most vulnerable. By guaranteeing that the 'cost' of the event (or the opportunity cost of not participating) is mitigated for excluded groups, the proposal moves from a potentially extractive awareness campaign to a genuinely equitable initiative. Consequently, the projected long-term impacts become more credible because they are no longer predicated on a 'halo effect' that ignores systemic barriers. The impact score should be revised to 4, reflecting a proposal that now actively manages distributional justice rather than obscuring it.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on highly optimistic and unverified assumptions regarding a 5% voluntary participation rate and the immediate cooperation of government and private sectors to modify bus routes and mandate home office. The technical feasibility is low due to the reliance on a custom 'Google-developed' app without a defined procurement or development pathway. The operational model assumes a 'big bang' event execution without secured MOUs or a concrete risk mitigation plan for logistical failures.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice, explicitly acknowledged by the proposal itself. The proposed innovation—a smartphone app to track steps and calculate carbon savings—is a standard feature in existing health and sustainability applications. The proposal repackages existing behaviors and technologies under a new event banner without introducing a novel mechanism for behavior change or a new technical architecture.

impact.rationale: The short-term emission savings (approx. 66 tCO2e/day) are plausible for a one-day event, and the proposal offers valid public health co-benefits for those able to walk or bike. However, the long-term impact projections were initially flawed due to causal overreach (attributing solar laws and bus electrification to a single day). Following expert clarification, the score reflects a revised understanding where these long-term outcomes are treated as 'aspirational goals' rather than direct causal results. The score is capped at 3 because the proposal still lacks a robust mechanism to ensure sustained behavioral change beyond the event day and retains significant risks regarding social equity (e.g., the 'skip the bus' instruction) unless specific inclusive alternatives are mandated.

presentation.rationale: The proposal suffers from a lack of scientific rigor and professional polish. It presents speculative forecasts as definitive outcomes, contains numerous grammatical errors, and relies on broken image references. The causal logic is presented linearly without acknowledging confounding variables, and the financial section lists cost categories without budget estimates. The narrative jumps between immediate logistics and distant, hypothetical policy outcomes, making the causal logic difficult to follow.

final.synthesis: The final judgment integrates the consensus that the proposal is a low-novelty, high-risk awareness campaign. Experts A1, A2, A3, A4, A5, and A6 unanimously agreed on the low Feasibility (2) and Novelty (2) scores, citing unverified dependencies and the repackaging of existing 'Bike to Work' concepts. The primary divergence occurred in the Impact dimension. Initially, scores ranged from 2 to 4. A1 and A6 penalized the proposal heavily for 'causal overreach' regarding long-term policy shifts. A4 and A5 raised concerns about social equity and the counter-productive advice to 'skip the bus.' Through the clarification process, the causal flaw was addressed by re-framing long-term goals as aspirational, which allowed A1 to raise their score to 3. However, A4 lowered their score to 2 due to the persistent equity risks, while A5 raised their score to 4 contingent on the inclusion of funded alternatives for excluded groups. The final Impact score of 3 represents a synthesis: it acknowledges the valid short-term health and emission benefits while penalizing the proposal for its lack of a concrete, inclusive strategy to ensure long-term habit formation and social equity. The Presentation score remains low (2) due to the consensus on the proposal's structural and grammatical weaknesses.
final.uncertainty: The primary uncertainty remains the actual participation rate and the efficacy of the 'awareness' to drive long-term habit change, as no pilot data exists. Additionally, the proposal's success is heavily dependent on external factors (government authorization, corporate cooperation, Google's involvement) that are currently unsecured. The final Impact score assumes that the 'aspirational' framing of long-term goals is sufficient to mitigate the causal overreach, but the lack of a detailed plan for sustaining behavioral change or addressing the 'skip the bus' equity issue leaves the long-term societal impact unproven. The score of 3 reflects a 'plausible but unproven' stance rather than a confident prediction of success.
